result:
I keep returning to the idea that everything is reheated starlight.  
The calcium that lets my fingers grip this pen, the iron that keeps my blood a respectable shade of crimson, the sodium that fires the neurons now pushing these syllables around—they were all forged in furnaces that went supernova long before the Sun was a glimmer in a nebula’s eye. When I look at my hand I am looking at a five-fingered autobiography of extinct suns. There is something hilarious and humiliating in that: galaxies died so I could bite my nails while waiting for a text message.

We moderns walk around like mobile scrapbooks of exploded constellations, yet we still get huffy when the Wi-Fi buffers. The incongruity makes me laugh until I remember I do exactly the same thing. The cosmos may have given me the carbon in my wristbones, but it did not install patience in the operating system.

I have been thinking about patience ever since I started learning to bake bread last winter. Bread is a quiet tyrant. You can cajole, threaten, or sweet-talk a tomato sauce, but bread does not negotiate. It rises on its own schedule, indifferent to your hunger, your dinner guests, or the movie that will be over in two hours. You are essentially renting time from a fungus—yes, yeast, I see you, globby minion—whose priorities are reproduction, alcohol, and carbon dioxide, in roughly that order. Standing at the counter with flour on my forearms, I feel like a landlord who has discovered the tenants are running an illegal bakery in the living room of my life.

The first loaf I produced could have been used as a foundation stone. I photographed it anyway, posted it with a jaunty caption, and received the digital applause we have all agreed is the appropriate reward for amateur achievement. The likes floated in, little dopamine bells, but the loaf itself remained obstinately brick-like. I ate a slice out of stubbornness, then used the rest as a doorstop. Picasso allegedly said that every act of creation is first an act of destruction; mine just happened to double as home-security hardware.

Bread is patient; people, less so. I keep meeting folks who treat relationships like toaster pastries: pop them in, expect instant warmth, preferably frosted. They speak of “emotional availability” the way McDonald’s speaks of drive-thru service—convenient, predictable, ninety seconds or the next one is free. They do not want to knead anyone’s loneliness for ten minutes, let alone let it rise overnight in the dark warmth of shared silence. I understand them because I am them. I, too, want intimacy with a two-minute setting. I, too, scroll through faces the way a bored emperor once flipped through scrolls of eligible virgins. The difference is his thumb left an ink blot; mine leaves a data trail sold to marketers who will try to sell me a meditation app promising “inner stillness in thirty seconds or less.” The algorithm knows I am spiritually impatient before I do.

Still, sometimes the universe sends a human who refuses to behave like an optimized user experience. Last month I rode a Greyhound from Austin to Shreveport because I had never been to Shreveport and because the absurdity of a nine-hour detour felt like the right speed for my restlessness. The seatmate lottery dealt me a woman named Dolores who carried a Tupperware of deviled eggs and an economy-sized bag of rubber bands.“For my wrists,” she explained, rolling one up each forearm like a boxer wrapping knuckles. “Keeps the swelling down.” She offered me an egg and told me, between bites, about her late husband who used to whistle entire Brahms symphonies while washing dishes. “He never finished one, though,” she said, “because the dishes got done too quick.” We laughed at the joke only marriage can understand: the defeat of love by efficiency. Somewhere around Marshall, Texas, she pulled out a tiny harmonica and played “Shenandoah” soft enough that only our seat row could hear. The sound was miniature, homesick, perfect. When we parted at the station she pressed two rubber bands into my palm. “For when you swell up,” she said. I have them still, coiled like tan snakes around the base of a cereal bowl, waiting for the day I might puff up with something—grief, maybe, or unexpected joy—and need to remember that strangers once offered makeshift tourniquets for the invisible bruises.

I rode back on Amtrak because I wanted to watch the country glide instead of lurch. Trains teach you the difference between velocity and speed: one is a number, the other a story. Outside the observation car the pine forests rearranged themselves patiently, like a slow-motion kaleidoscope operated by an omnipotent child with all the time in the world. I thought about how trees practice a form of socialism: they share sugar with the sick, warn each other of insect attacks, leave their leaves as collective compost. Meanwhile humans hoard bottled water during hurricanes. Somewhere north of Little Rock the café car ran out of coffee and the outrage was operatic. A man in a seersucker suit threatened to sue. I wanted to tell him that the universe started with an explosion and now survives on drip, but I kept quiet; even prophets know when the audience is caffeinated enough to throw things.

Back home I tried another loaf. This time I let it rise while I rode the bike trail that skirts the Trinity River. Herons stood in the shallows like contemplative monks who had taken a vow of fishing. A kid on a BMX asked me if I had seen his turtle; he had painted a lightning bolt on its shell so it would “go faster.” I helped him look for twenty minutes. We never found the turtle, but he told me about his plans to become both a DJ and an astronaut—”so I can drop beats in zero G.” His optimism tasted better than any prospective sourdough. When I returned, the dough had doubled, arrogant as a billionaire. I punched it down, feeling like a benevolent tyrant. The second loaf emerged with the proper crumb, the ear-splitting crackle when it cooled, the interior like a honeycombed sunrise. I sliced it, buttered it, took a bite, and felt—nothing. It was just bread. Good bread, yes, but the miracle I expected failed to sync with my mouth. I realized I had been anticipating salvation, not carbohydrates. The bread had fulfilled its contract; I had misread the fine print.

That night I sat on the porch and watched heat lightning unzip the Texas sky without sound. Somewhere above the stratosphere photons that left Vega in 1984 were finally arriving, anonymous tourists. I thought about how every faith tradition has a bread story: unleavened haste, manna from heaven, loaves that multiply like venture capital. We keep hoping the earth’s most basic grain will rise into something that can outrun mortality. Maybe the real miracle is that we keep trying to feed each other at all. Dolores’s deviled egg, the boy’s missing turtle, the heron’s stillness—all small loaves cast onto the waters, returning as best they can.

Tomorrow I will mix flour, water, salt, and the same obedient fungus. The dough will rise; I will wait; the planet will turn. The stars that gave me these elements are already dead, but their light is still en route. Enough of it reaches my retinas to keep me from walking into walls, literal and metaphorical. Meanwhile, somewhere in Shreveport, Dolores is probably wrapping another rubber band around a wrist that remembers more than it hurts. The boy’s turtle is crawling south at whatever speed enlightenment travels when it carries its house on its back. And the harmonica note that slipped between bus seats continues to vibrate, a tiny wave in an ocean of waves, indifferent to applause, rising and falling like dough in a warm kitchen that smells, for a moment, almost like home.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 1000 words.
